cuisine: Western Café — Singapore (café breakfast/brunch staple; influenced by Swiss Bircher muesli tradition)
region_of_origin: Switzerland (Bircher muesli origin, ~1900); adopted into Singapore specialty café scene from ~2015–2016
food_subgroup: Cold-prepared overnight rolled oats with Greek yoghurt, chia seeds, and banana; resistant starch advantage

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for overnight oats as a prepared dish. Closest parent concept: oat-based cold cereal preparation. FoodOn URI left null pending formal ontology extension.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cold-prepared overnight oats with Greek yoghurt, chia seeds, and fruit — Singapore café style
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0OAC (oat-based cereal preparation) used as closest parent; no exact code for overnight cold-prepared oats with yoghurt and chia.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore café serving is one jar of approximately 300g, comprising rolled oats 45g, almond milk ~120ml, Greek yoghurt ~80g, chia seeds 10g, honey ~7g, and banana slices ~40g, prepared cold overnight.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — rolled oats (FDC 173904), Greek yoghurt (FDC 171304), chia seeds (FDC 12006), banana, almond milk; weighted composite calculation for overnight oats dish
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central component entries for rolled oats (FDC 173904), plain Greek yoghurt (FDC 171304), chia seeds (FDC 12006), banana, almond milk, and honey. Per-100g values calculated from the 300g jar recipe (rolled oats 45g + almond milk 120ml + Greek yoghurt 80g + chia seeds 10g + honey 7g + banana 40g = ~302g total, normalised to 300g). Resistant starch retrogradation effect on GI documented in peer-reviewed food science literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of oat-based foods — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on resistant starch retrogradation in cold-prepared rolled oats
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed food science literature (resistant starch retrogradation)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~47 estimated for overnight cold-prepared rolled oats based on University of Sydney GI Database reference GI for rolled oats (~55 hot-cooked) adjusted downward by approximately 8–10 GI units to reflect resistant starch retrogradation during overnight cold soaking, as documented in peer-reviewed food science literature. Chia seed gel and Greek yoghurt protein further moderate postprandial glucose response. GL per 300g serving = (47 × 48g available carbohydrate) / 100 = ~22.6; input record states 21.2 (minor rounding difference; 21.2 retained from source record). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~195mg per 300g jar) and beta-glucan content from rolled oats
hypertension.guidance: With only ~65mg sodium per 100g (~195mg per jar), overnight oats is one of the lowest-sodium café breakfast options available; beta-glucan from rolled oats (~1.6g per jar) contributes to modest blood pressure reduction via gut microbiome-mediated short-chain fatty acid production when consumed as part of a diet achieving ≥3g beta-glucan daily.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~47) from resistant starch retrogradation, chia gel, Greek yoghurt protein, and beta-glucan — four concurrent glycaemic-modulating mechanisms
type2_diabetes.guidance: GI ~47 and GL ~21 per jar represent one of the most favourable glycaemic profiles available in a Singapore café breakfast; the combination of resistant starch retrogradation (cold preparation), chia seed gel (slows gastric emptying), Greek yoghurt protein (moderates insulin response), and oat beta-glucan viscosity provides four concurrent mechanisms for postprandial glucose control, making this dish suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion is limited to one jar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beta-glucan (LDL-lowering), omega-3 ALA from chia seeds, low saturated fat (~2.4g per jar), and very low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oat beta-glucan (~1.6g per jar) contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction (FDA-qualified health claim at ≥3g/day); omega-3 ALA from chia seeds (~2.8g per 10g serving) supports cardiovascular health; saturated fat is low at ~0.8g per 100g (~2.4g per jar); very low sodium (~195mg per jar) supports blood pressure management — collectively making this dish a cardioprotective café breakfast choice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory nutrients: omega-3 ALA (chia), beta-glucan (prebiotic), Greek yoghurt probiotics, low sodium, calcium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Anti-inflammatory profile includes omega-3 ALA (~2.8g from chia seeds), beta-glucan prebiotic supporting gut microbiome-mediated anti-inflammatory pathways, Greek yoghurt probiotics, very low sodium, and ~285mg calcium per jar relevant for corticosteroid-associated osteoporosis prevention in RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Overnight Oats — Singapore Café Style is a cold-prepared breakfast dish served in a jar (~300g) consisting of rolled oats (45g) soaked overnight in almond milk (~120ml) with plain Greek yoghurt (~80g), chia seeds (10g), honey (~7g), and banana slices (~40g). No cooking is involved; the oats are hydrated and softened by cold soaking in the refrigerator overnight. The texture is creamy and thick, with a mild sweetness from honey and banana, a slight tang from Greek yoghurt, and a gel-like quality from chia seeds. This format was introduced to Singapore's specialty café scene from approximately 2015–2016, adapted from the Swiss Bircher muesli tradition (developed by Dr. Maximilian Oskar Bircher-Benner ~1900 in Zurich). Per 300g jar: approximately 324 kcal, 17.4g protein, 9.6g fat, 48g carbohydrate, 8.4g dietary fibre, ~195mg sodium (very low). GI ~47 (low) — lower than hot-cooked rolled oats (~55) due to resistant starch retrogradation during cold overnight soaking. GL ~21 per jar (medium). Four concurrent glycaemic-modulating mechanisms: resistant starch retrogradation, chia gel, Greek yoghurt protein, and oat beta-glucan viscosity. Suitable for Type 2 Diabetes, Hypertension, and Cardiovascular Disease. Must use rolled oats — instant oats (GI ~83) eliminate the resistant starch benefit. Source: USDA FoodData Central composite (FDC 173904, 171304, 12006). Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: is overnight oats better than regular oats

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (FDC 173904 rolled oats, FDC 171304 Greek yoghurt, FDC 12006 chia seeds, banana, almond milk) — all approved primary sources. GI estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database reference for rolled oats with documented resistant starch retrogradation adjustment from peer-reviewed literature. All mandatory multilingual names present for Singaporean cuisine category. Confidence 0.9 on GI (analogous estimate, not direct overnight oats GI measurement); all other fields high confidence.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite overnight oats dish is not individually reported in USDA FoodData Central for the combined preparation; component-level data exists but dish-level composite fatty acid split not available as a single verified entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the combined cold-soaked preparation is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central component data for all ingredients in this preparation. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for rolled oats, Greek yoghurt (plain, low-fat), chia seeds, banana, and unsweetened almond milk without vitamin D fortification. GI value of 47 is an analogous estimate: University of Sydney GI Database reference GI for rolled oats (~55, hot-cooked) adjusted downward ~8–10 units for resistant starch retrogradation in cold overnight preparation per peer-reviewed food science literature; confidence 0.9. GL per serving calculated as (47 × 48g available carbohydrate per 300g jar) / 100 = ~22.6; value of 21.2 retained from source record (minor rounding difference). Dish is suitable for halal and vegetarian diets; not vegan due to Greek yoghurt and honey. Rolled oats must be used — instant oats (GI ~83) eliminate the resistant starch retrogradation benefit.
